<!-- Topic 1: Why Pointers? -->
<!-- Slides 3–13 -->

# Why Pointers?
<!-- Slide 3 -->

## What Can an Address Do? {.smaller}

- A variable gives a value a name
- A pointer makes the value's location usable data

::: notes
Slides 3–13
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## The Symbol Table Remembers Addresses

| Name | Type | Scope | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `score` | `int` | `main` | `0x16F...A4` |
| `average` | `double` | `main` | `0x16F...98` |

- The symbol table connects a source-code name to information about its storage
- The displayed addresses are conceptual; compilation and execution determine the actual locations

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Names Lead to Memory

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
```

`score` → symbol-table entry → address → `88`

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: draw the four-stage chain with an arrow from the symbol-table address to a memory cell containing 88.]
:::

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Ordinary Access Starts With a Name

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int score = 88;
    cout << score << "\n";
    score = 95;
    cout << score << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Pointers Preserve the Address

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int score = 88;
    int* ptr = &score;

    cout << ptr << "\n";
    cout << *ptr << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Two Paths to the Same Variable

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* ptr = &score;

score = 90;
cout << *ptr << "\n";  // 90

*ptr = 95;
cout << score << "\n"; // 95
```

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## Names Are Fixed; Pointers Can Choose

```{.cpp}
int regularPrice = 40;
int salePrice = 28;
bool saleIsActive = true;

int* selected = &regularPrice;
if (saleIsActive) {
    selected = &salePrice;
}

cout << *selected << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## Addresses Can Cross Boundaries

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};
int* beginning = &values[0];
int* third = &values[2];
```

- An address can be stored for later access
- The same address can describe an array position or cross a function boundary

<!-- Slide 11 -->

---

## Why Add Indirection?

- Select an object while the program runs
- Modify an existing object through another access path
- Traverse contiguous data without copying it
- Prepare for interfaces and memory whose location is known only at runtime

<!-- Slide 12 -->

---

## Pointers Extend the Memory Model

`name` → `address` → `pointer` → `object`

- A pointer does not replace the object
- It makes the object's location programmable

<!-- Slide 13 -->
